4.2. Reasons for using mobile devices
The study participants decided on the use of their MobDs in order to learn English for
the reason that they regarded them as convenient, fast and always ready to use. In
addition, some students pointed to the fact that the use of MobDs allowed them to have
quick access to the internet and organize their own study materials and/or resources.
Illustrative examples of such opinions are provided below (3):
S10: It’s very comfortable. I can reach for my dictionary any time I want and I
don’t have to carry thick books (...) The main aspect is convenience.
S5: It’s because I can find needed information ... it’s convenient because I
always carry my smartphone and I have access to the internet all the time (...)
At home I also use my smartphone and I don’t mind it has a small screen.
S14: My tablet lets me organize things and keep my documents in one place.
This is because studying English means having countless study materials (...) I
can store them there (...) this also gives me easier access to them (...) In
addition, my smartphone can successfully replace a traditional paper dictionary
and I don’t have to waste time in thumbing through a lot of pages to find words
I’m looking for.
4.3. Resources and tools
The analysis of the data revealed that the students made use of both online resources
and mobile apps. The most frequently used language tools were online dictionaries
(e.g. diki, ColorDict Dictionary) and a variety of mobile apps, such as Google
Translate, Duolingo and Fiszkoteka. The students usually accessed these tools in order
to check, revise and learn the target language vocabulary. Two students also reported
using Voscreen and WhatsApp, i.e. mobile apps for watching video and communicating
with people, respectively. It should also be noted that the interviewees pointed out
various online resources they used with the purpose of practicing reading and listening
skills (e.g. TED, online newspapers, YouTube), vocabulary (e.g. 6 Minute
23
The EUROCALL Review, Volume 25, No. 2, September 2017
English, PONS, Google Translate) and having access to language materials
(e.g. Academica). Finally, some students used their MobDs in order to read language
materials downloaded from the internet (e.g. PDF files). The following responses
illustrate some of these issues:
S5: I use apps for “index cards”, dictionaries and a variety of apps for
developing English vocabulary.
S10: I have some online friends and I talk with them in English (Do you do this
by means of instant messaging applications?) Yes, I use WhatsApp Messenger.
S15: I often read scanned book pages and pdf materials (…) I access English
vocabulary by means of online dictionaries.
S20: Fiszkoteka. I frequently use this app (...) I also listen to podcasts and I
have the app called Six minutes English in order to practice listening (...) Also
because vocabulary is used in a variety of contexts.
4.4. Mobile encounters
Thirteen (65%) interviewees claimed to use their MobDs most frequently in their leisure
time, six (30%) in the classroom and one student said he had used his smartphone
equally frequently in the classroom and out-of-class English study. As for the students
who used their smartphones or tablet computers in their leisure time, some of them did
it with the aim of reading English texts, listening to audio resources, checking and
learning new vocabulary, preparing multimedia presentations and playing language
games. This is not to say, of course, that this group of learners did not use their MobDs
at all during classes; however, the use of MobDs in this respect was only limited to
checking target language vocabulary (e.g. S1: I use my smartphone, for example, to
check something I don’t understand (...) I installed a dictionary and I use it to find
words). When it comes to the subjects who claimed to use their MobDs most frequently
in the classroom, they used them to check unfamiliar vocabulary and/or find words they
needed during various language activities. It is also important to note that these
students were not very willing to use their MobDs at home in view of the fact that they
favored their home computers. For example:
S7: I use them outside of University in order to learn and practice English
vocabulary and to prepare multimedia presentations.
S13: In my free time I learn English words and phrases, listen to English
recordings and I read various texts in English.
S19: Yes, I use my smartphone and tablet for out-of-class learning but I also
use them during classes mostly to check words and collocations.
S16: I think I do this during practical English language classes more regularly in
the classroom than outside of it (...) In the classroom I check English words in
digital dictionaries (...) I do this to check words, spelling, or to recall some words
(...) or I use my smartphone to look for synonyms (...).
The analysis of the gathered data also demonstrated that the majority of the
interviewees (13 or 65%) were in favor of using their smartphones and/or tablet
computers for informal English learning (i.e. learning the target language for pleasure)
and 7 (35%) students associated the use of their MobDs with formal learning (i.e.
related to their studies). It should be noted, however, that only two interviewees
claimed to hold and somewhat organize regular mobile English language sessions:
S10: I think this is what I have talked about earlier, I mean these chats with my
friends. Perhaps we don’t chat very regularly ... we chat three times a week and
that’s it but, at the same time, it’s not sporadic because we arrange it and it
takes place pretty regularly.
S16: I often watch videos on YouTube and I do this the most often through my
smartphone.
Finally, it has to be noted that the use of mobile devices was not explicitly advised or
suggested by the interviewees’ teachers during their practical English language classes
or any other classes at the university. This is not to say, of course, that they never
24
The EUROCALL Review, Volume 25, No. 2, September 2017
referred their students to electronic or online resources; however, they did not ask
students to use them in classes, they did not recommend any mobile apps or design
language tasks which required using such devices in order to solve them.
4.5. Language practiced
When asked to indicate the most frequently practiced language skills and subsystems by
means of mobile devices, all the interviewees indicated the target language vocabulary.
In addition to this, some referred to pronunciation and only a few students mentioned
grammar and practicing reading, listening and speaking skills. As far as practicing
English vocabulary is concerned, the subjects chose to practice it through their
smartphones and/or tables because they regarded this language subsystem as the most
important to learn, they praised their MobDs for providing them with quick and easy
access to needed words and see the way they were used in given sentences. As was
stressed by many of the interviewees, learning English vocabulary by means of mobile
devices also allowed them to check correct pronunciation of words (i.e. they listened to
it or paid attention to phonetic transcription of words). The following excerpts exemplify
the most typical usage of MobDs by the study participants:
S3: (…) as for vocabulary I guess it’s much faster to search for words and know
how to use them in sentences.
S6: It’s easy and it’s very easy to look for words when I need them.
S12: (…) I need vocabulary not only to communicate in English (…) when I look
for words I look at contexts words are used (...) I always pay attention to
spelling and also listen to pronunciation (How about phonetic transcription of
words?) Phonetic transcription of words ... yes but not often unless audio is poor
quality or it seems to sound somehow differently ... then I make sure how a
word is pronounced and I read its phonetic transcription given there.
As mentioned earlier, only a few students resorted to their MobDs in order to practice
other language areas such as listening, reading and speaking as well as grammar. This
is because they preferred more traditional resources (e.g. grammar books), they used
other devices (e.g. laptop computers) or they regarded themselves as quite proficient in
particular language skills and thus they did not feel the need to master them by way of
MobDs. Representative excerpts from the interviewees’ responses follow:
S3: When it comes to grammar, for me it’s more convenient to use grammar
books to learn it.
S2: (…) I’m pretty good at English grammar and listening and I don’t have to
use my smartphone to learn these language elements.
S12: I think I’m quite good at grammar and I practice listening skills by means
of my laptop computer.
4.6. Study performance
There is evidence that the use of mobile devices became an impetus for studying
English more and learn this language more effectively and efficiently (this advantageous
effect was expressed by as many as 15 or 75% interviewees). This is because access to
a smartphone or a tablet allows some learners devote more time to learning English
(S1: Yes, I think so. I think I spend more time ... If I was to use traditional materials,
for example, books, I wouldn’t devote so much time to it.; S15: It seems to me that I
dedicate more time to learn English this way and I learn more.), encouraged another
student to learn more (S6: I’m more willing to use my smartphone than open a paper
dictionary.) and allowed yet another subject to learn more vocabulary (S12: Yes,
definitely. I wouldn’t have learned these words if I hadn’t used my phone.). Such
beneficial outcomes of the use of MobDs are best described by one of the interviewees
who said:
If I’m to say that I devote more time for learning English it’s because I can
devote more time to learning it ... in the way I compare a paper dictionary with
an online one ... for example to check one word ... If I use a traditional
dictionary it takes me longer, say three minutes, but If I use an online dictionary
it takes me, say, ten seconds (...) this way I can devote less time to looking for
25
The EUROCALL Review, Volume 25, No. 2, September 2017
information and more on language production, on the use of English ... there is
less time used but it’s more effective. (S14)
It is also interesting to note that the use of mobile devices might be valuable for
kinesthetic or tactical language learners:
I think I spend more time … for me it’s much nicer and more interesting than
sitting and reading books … it’s better for me since I’m kinesthetic so it’s hard
for me to sit and read a traditional book ... it’s because I don’t remember then
much but when I use my smartphone which is mobile I can ... I can do it while
doing other activities and this makes things easier for me. (S5)
Finally, it should be noted that 5 (25%) interviewees were not able to say whether or
not the use of mobile devices made them study the target language more effectively or
efficiently and they expressed their opinion by simply claiming that “It’s difficult to say”.
5. Discussion and conclusions
The picture that emerges from the analysis of the collected data regarding the advanced
learners' use of mobile devices for learning English is relatively encouraging. This is
because all the study participants used, at least to some extent, their mobile devices
(i.e. smartphones and/or tablet computers) in order to learn the English language
autonomously. Moreover, the positive impact of using mobile devices for English study
was acknowledged by the majority of the interviewees. Their beneficial contribution to
their English development was chiefly linked with easy access to English language
resources, the opportunity to store them, comfort in using their smartphones and
tablets anywhere and anytime as well as perceived gains in English learning. The results
of the study also showed that all interviewees engaged with their smartphones and/or
tablet computers to practice the target language vocabulary (plus some students also
claimed to learn pronunciation of English words) and the majority of the subjects used
their mobile devices autonomously in their leisure time as well as during language
classes. Such a state of affairs can be explained in terms of increased awareness on the
part of some students of the beneficial role of MobDs in foreign language learning, their
ability to reach for appropriate tools and retrieve needed information to achieve their
goals and adjust their learning of the target language to their personal learning styles.
Despite this positive view of MobDs reported by the study participants, the results of the
study also revealed that only a few subjects engaged with their mobile devices to
master target language skills such as reading, listening, writing and speaking as well as
English language grammar. In addition, some interviewees limited themselves to a
rather intuitive and perhaps even spontaneous use of their mobile devices in the
language classroom. It should also be noted that almost half of the subjects regarded
themselves as quite inexperienced in using their mobile devices when it comes to
learning the English language despite the fact that some of the students had been using
them with the intention of learning English for years. Taking all these findings into
account, one may conclude that this is due to a failure or underestimation of the role
and place of mobile devices in foreign language learning and teaching on the part of
language teachers. It seems therefore warranted to say that the subjects’ use of mobile
devices could be altered if teachers took into account the benefits they may offer. For
this reason language teachers should, for instance, present the affordances of mobile
technology and discuss them with students during language classes. They should also
select mobile apps and create opportunities for using them in- and out-of-class learning
by offering or designing tasks devoted to practicing a variety of language skills and
subsystems suitable for the use of such devices. If this were to happen, teachers need
to respond quickly to the constant and dynamic changes in contemporary
foreign/second language learning and teaching contexts by undergoing official teacher
training not only in the area of technology-mediated language learning and teaching but
also in the context of learner autonomy.
As with all studies, the study reported in this paper has some limitations. Although the
interviewees represented a range of experience of English language learning, the small
number of participants reduces the generalizability of the results. Another limitation is
related to the fact that the group was largely homogenous, i.e. the subjects came from
the same institution and all studied English. Yet another weakness may concern the
26
The EUROCALL Review, Volume 25, No. 2, September 2017
data collection instrument, namely the semi-structured interview which was conducted
only once. Perhaps a different set of questions, their wording or a series of such
interviews carried out over a particular period of time (say one academic year) may
have yielded more detailed and insightful results. Despite these limitations, this study
provided some insights into why and how advanced English language learners engage
with their mobile devices to develop learning experiences. It should be stressed,
however, that teacher involvement in creating conditions conducive to the use of mobile
devices for language study may result in greater learner engagement with mobile
technology (i.e. mobile devices) and, at the same time, may lead to greater students’
independence in learning the target language.

The gathered data were subjected to qualitative and quantitative analysis. The results of the study demonstrated that, on the one hand, some subjects manifested heightened awareness relating to the advantageous role of mobile devices in their learning endeavors, their ability to reach for suitable tools and retrieve necessary information so as to achieve their goals, meet their needs and adjust their learning of English to their personal learning styles, and on the other, a rather intuitive and/or ad hoc use of their mobile devices in the classroom.

It should be noted, however, that this link and “future enquiry and practice into technology-mediated learner autonomy will need to be increasingly aligned to the tools, settings, and activities that are of significance to language learners” (Reinders & White, 2016, p. 151). Reinders and White (2016) further argue that as long as “the potential range of settings, tools, and experiences is now virtually limitless,
sections.individuals need to be increasingly adep: from and contribute effectively to those settings” (p. 151). Beyond doubt, contemporary language teachers should equip foreign/second language learners with appropriate
sections.knowledge concerning the affordances of: should prepare them for effective usage of such devices for this purpose. It is also of paramount importance, for both researchers and practitioners, to comprehend the link between the modalities of the language learners' organization of their own learning experiences and environments and the role mobile technologies, in particular smartphones and tablets, play in these contexts. Taking into consideration the above-mentioned issues, the study reported in this article investigated ways advanced English language students use their mobile devices (i.e. smartphones and tablet computers) for their language learning. The article commences with a short overview of relevant literature. Next, the design of the study is described,

sections.2.1. Autonomy in foreign/second language: The concept of autonomy in second/foreign language learning and teaching has been
sections.the focus of attention for many research: decades. According to Benson (2001), the notion of autonomy was introduced and popularized in 1981 by Henri Holec in his seminal report for the Council of Europe
sections.entitled Autonomy in Foreign Language Le: autonomy in the context of language learning as “the ability to take charge of one’s own learning” (Holec, 1981, p. 3). Holec’s idea of autonomy encompasses some components and capacities on the part of language learners (e.g. self-directed learning). For some other authors autonomy also involves “a capacity – for detachment, critical reflection, decision-making, and independent action” (Little, 1991, p. 4) and “the capacity to take control of one’s own learning” (Benson, 2001, p. 46). As stated by Benson (2011, p.
sections.16), “autonomy is multidimensional and t: person, the setting, and multiple contextual and micro-contextual factors” and it is “a multi-faceted concept that consists of several layers” (Reinders, 2011, p. 48) whose roots are based in political, societal and educational developments. In addition to this,
sections.work on autonomy emphasizes social dimen: fact that “autonomous learners always do things for themselves, but they may or may not do things on their own” (Little, 2009, p. 223) and that by means of social interactions language learners “develop a capacity to analyze, reflect upon and synthesize information to create new perspectives” (Lee, 2011, p. 88). It should also be
sections.noted that recent research shows that fo: matter of individualizing learning through out-of-class initiatives since the dominance of classroom-based approaches (Benson, 2011). Finally, it has to be added that researchers, in general, seem to be in agreement with the following claims suggested by Benson (2011): “(a) language learners naturally tend to take control of their learning, (b) learners who lack autonomy are capable of developing it, and (c) autonomous language learning is more effective than non-autonomous language learning” (p. 16).
sections.Perhaps in order to define the concept o: to understand who autonomous learners are. As Littlewood (1996) claims, an autonomous learner is “one who has independent capacity to make and carry out choices which govern his or her actions” (p. 428). The researcher argues that this capacity depends on two major components such as ability and willingness, and he claims that the attributes can also be further subdivided. Thus, ability depends on
sections.having knowledge about the options from: choose the most suitable alternatives. When it comes to willingness, this depends on having motivation and confidence in order to take responsibility for adequate choices. Moreover, Littlewood (1996) argues that if an individual is to be successful in being autonomous, all of these components have to be present all together. At the close of this section, a few words are in order on the notion of autonomous language learning. An interesting description of the concept in question is offered by Reinders (2011), who defines autonomous language learning as “an act of learning whereby motivated learners consciously make informed decisions about that learning” (p. 48). According to the said researcher, it is not possible or needed in all acts of
sections.learning to be able or ready to intentio: situations present different demands. Reinders (2011) further argues that “autonomy is not an either-or concept, but has to be seen as a continuum” (p. 48). This is because a learner can display more or less autonomy in different learning circumstances. Autonomy, in Reinders’ terms changes over time between skills and within skills and thus it is difficult to achieve and is not invariably permanent (Reinders, 2011).

sections.2.2. Autonomy and new technologies: As stated in the previous section, the concept of autonomy has been one of the most researched areas in the field of second/foreign language learning and teaching over the last few decades. It should be noted, however, that the field of learner autonomy started to be influenced by technology in the mid-1990s as a result of the growing influence of the internet on almost every sphere of our life (including second/foreign language education) and the opportunities for online collaboration and communication (Reinders & White, 2016). As stated by Benson and Chik (2010), the latest generations of new technologies, particularly those encompassing the internet, user-generated Web content and mobility, seem to be having a bearing on the way autonomous language learning develops. Perhaps, one of the most important benefits of implementing new
sections.technologies into language learning is t: learners who do not have direct access to the target language. This is because the use of new technologies, including mobile technology, allow them to “bypass classrooms and go directly to target language texts and users through the internet and social media” (Benson, 2011, p. 17). When it comes to the use of mobile technology, and, in particular, smartphones and tablet computers, for learning a foreign or second language, they can assist language
sections.learners with their learning endeavors b: resources whenever and wherever such learners need them and/or they happen to be (Jones, 2015). In Jones’s opinion, such language involvement might comprise, for instance, the use of chunks of spare time for language practice, searching for target language vocabulary in relevant contexts or interactions on social media (Jones, 2015). Furthermore, by having a mobile device a language learner has the opportunity to take control of his or her learning, direct it and engage in language activities that meet his or her individual needs and goals (Kukulska-Hulme, Traxler & Pettit, 2007; Pettit & Kukulska-Hulme 2007). Given the importance attached to new technologies, and, in particular the potential role of mobile devices in autonomous language learning, the terms mobile learning and mobile devices (MobDs) need first to be explained. As for mobile learning, no single agreed-upon definition of the term exists in the literature (Oz, 2015). This is because some researchers define mobile learning as an extension of e-learning built
sections.upon mobile devices whereas some other r: happens anywhere and anytime (cf. Oz, 2015). As far as mobile devices are concerned, they can be defined as “any device that is small, autonomous and unobtrusive enough to accompany us in every moment and can be used for educational purposes” (Trifanova Knapp, Ronchetti & Gamper, 2004, p. 3) or as “hand held electronic devices that can be comfortably carried around in a pocket or bag, including MP3 players, digital recorders, e-readers, tablets, and smartphones” (Kukulska-Hulme, Norris & Donohue,
sections.A lot of studies concerning the use of m: learning have been published. The findings of these studies concentrated on, for example, language learners’ views on the use of mobile devices in language instruction (e.g. Oz, 2015), students’ attitudes towards using mobile phones as instructional tools for foreign language learning (e.g. Cakir, 2015), profiling mobile language learners (e.g. Byrne & Diem, 2014), their effect on learning a foreign/second language (e.g. Nah, White & Sussex, 2008; Cavus & Ibrahim, 2009; Zhang, Song & Burston, 2011), distance language learning (e.g. Demouy, Jones, Kan, Kukulska-Hulme & Eardley,
sections.2016), informal language learning practi: learners’ use of mobile devices for learning a foreign language (Stockwell, 2007; Dashtestani, 2015) and autonomy in language learning (e.g. Díaz-Vera, 2012; Djoub,
sections.devices and presented both benefits and: (e.g. Miangah & Nezarat, 2012), discussed the use of mobile devices in supporting social contacts and collaborative learning (e.g. Kukulska-Hulme & Shield, 2008) and offered guidelines related to the implementation of mobile learning into second/foreign language instruction (e.g. Kukulska-Hulme et al., 2015).

sections.3.1. Research question: One of the questions related to future research and practice in technology-mediated
sections.learner autonomy addressed by Reinders a: the relationship between technology and autonomy in the journal Language Learning &
sections.environments in order to develop learnin: their needs as language learners (Reinders & White, 2016). Taking this important matter into consideration, and in view of the fact that mobile technology, including mobile devices such as smartphones and tablet computers, are ubiquitous and substantial constituents of almost every language learner’s everyday life, the abovementioned question was modified and posed in this study in the following way:
sections.Do students engage with their mobile dev: (e.g. the use of mobile devices for formal and/or informal English language study) that meet their needs and goals (e.g. the development of the target language skills and sub-skills) as English language learners? If yes, why and how do they do this?
sections.3.2. Participants: The participants were 20 Polish university students of English philology, nine of whom (seven females and two males) were in the second year of their BA programme, six (five females and one male) in the third year and five (all females) in the second year of their MA program (1). The study participants were on average 22.22 years old (20.66 - year 2, BA; 21.82 - year 3, BA and 24.50 - year 2, MA). The subjects reported having learned English for an average of 11.38 years (10.49 - year 2, BA; 11.27 - year 3, BA and 12.21 - year 2, MA). The proficiency level represented by the participants of the study could be described as somewhere between B1 and B2 (second year BA students), B2 and C1 (third year BA students) and C1 and C2 (second year MA students), as
sections.3.3. Data collection and analysis: The data were gathered by means of a semi-structured interview. This interview format
sections.and prompts and interviewees are encoura: during it (Dörnyei, 2007). As Dörnyei (2007) explains, in this type of the interview “the interviewer provides guidelines and direction (hence the ‘-structured’ part in the name),
sections.but is also keen to follow up interestin: elaborate on certain issues (hence the ‘semi-’ part)” (p. 136). During the interview, the present researcher attempted to encourage the subjects to
sections.describe their learning experiences conc: study. This was a form of introspection where the students were prompted to examine their behaviors and provide a first person narrative of such experiences. All the study
sections.participants were informed that the inte: English study and they were asked for permission to be digitally recorded. In order to obtain relevant data the following questions were asked (2):  Do you use your mobile devices (e.g. smartphones, tablets, MP3 players,
sections.PDAs) for learning English?:  Why do you use your mobile device(s) for learning English?  When did you start using your mobile device(s) for learning English? Has the use of your mobile device(s) increased or decreased since that time?  Do you use your mobile device(s) during classes?  Do you use your mobile device(s) more frequently in- or out-of-class English learning?  What mobile apps have you been using most frequently and/or recently?  Do you use your mobile device(s) more often in relation to formal or informal English language study?
sections.the data were read several times in orde: occurring information. The recurring ideas were coded and recoded, revised and updated. The researcher used the highlighting function of the word processor program
sections.which allows the user to highlight the t: comments to record any observations and thematic categories recognized in the data. The emerged categories were reviewed, compared, modified and either merged or abandoned. It should also be noted that the obtained data were analyzed quantitatively. This type of analysis involved counting the number of the interviewees’ responses and calculating percentages.
sections.4. Findings: A thorough analysis of the data yielded the following thematic categories: usage of mobile devices, reasons for using mobile devices, resources and tools, mobile encounters, language practiced and study performance.
sections.4.1. Usage of mobile devices: Table 1 shows the study participants’ mobile devices (MobDs) usage descriptions. The
sections.table demonstrates that smartphones were: students. In addition, the numerical information in the table indicates that the participants, on average, had been using them for English language study for about
started using their mobile devices at senior high school and university, respectively. It should also be added that, with the exception of one student (i.e. S9), all the other
sections.participants claimed to use their mobile: frequently with time. Finally, more than half of the students (55%) regarded themselves as experienced or fairly experienced users of their MobDs for English language learning; however, 45% claimed not to be very skilful in this area. sections.The study participants decided on the us: the reason that they regarded them as convenient, fast and always ready to use. In addition, some students pointed to the fact that the use of MobDs allowed them to have quick access to the internet and organize their own study materials and/or resources.
sections.Illustrative examples of such opinions a: S10: It’s very comfortable. I can reach for my dictionary any time I want and I don’t have to carry thick books (...) The main aspect is convenience. S5: It’s because I can find needed information ... it’s convenient because I always carry my smartphone and I have access to the internet all the time (...) At home I also use my smartphone and I don’t mind it has a small screen. S14: My tablet lets me organize things and keep my documents in one place. This is because studying English means having countless study materials (...) I can store them there (...) this also gives me easier access to them (...) In addition, my smartphone can successfully replace a traditional paper dictionary and I don’t have to waste time in thumbing through a lot of pages to find words I’m looking for.
sections.The analysis of the data revealed that t: and mobile apps. The most frequently used language tools were online dictionaries (e.g. diki, ColorDict Dictionary) and a variety of mobile apps, such as Google Translate, Duolingo and Fiszkoteka. The students usually accessed these tools in order to check, revise and learn the target language vocabulary. Two students also reported using Voscreen and WhatsApp, i.e. mobile apps for watching video and communicating with people, respectively. It should also be noted that the interviewees pointed out
sections.various online resources they used with: skills (e.g. TED, online newspapers, YouTube), vocabulary (e.g. 6 Minute
sections.23: The EUROCALL Review, Volume 25, No. 2, September 2017
sections.English, PONS, Google Translate) and hav: (e.g. Academica). Finally, some students used their MobDs in order to read language materials downloaded from the internet (e.g. PDF files). The following responses illustrate some of these issues: S5: I use apps for “index cards”, dictionaries and a variety of apps for developing English vocabulary. S10: I have some online friends and I talk with them in English (Do you do this by means of instant messaging applications?) Yes, I use WhatsApp Messenger. S15: I often read scanned book pages and pdf materials (…) I access English vocabulary by means of online dictionaries. S20: Fiszkoteka. I frequently use this app (...) I also listen to podcasts and I have the app called Six minutes English in order to practice listening (...) Also because vocabulary is used in a variety of contexts.
sections.4.4. Mobile encounters: Thirteen (65%) interviewees claimed to use their MobDs most frequently in their leisure time, six (30%) in the classroom and one student said he had used his smartphone equally frequently in the classroom and out-of-class English study. As for the students who used their smartphones or tablet computers in their leisure time, some of them did it with the aim of reading English texts, listening to audio resources, checking and learning new vocabulary, preparing multimedia presentations and playing language games. This is not to say, of course, that this group of learners did not use their MobDs at all during classes; however, the use of MobDs in this respect was only limited to checking target language vocabulary (e.g. S1: I use my smartphone, for example, to check something I don’t understand (...) I installed a dictionary and I use it to find words). When it comes to the subjects who claimed to use their MobDs most frequently in the classroom, they used them to check unfamiliar vocabulary and/or find words they needed during various language activities. It is also important to note that these
sections.students were not very willing to use th: favored their home computers. For example: S7: I use them outside of University in order to learn and practice English vocabulary and to prepare multimedia presentations. S13: In my free time I learn English words and phrases, listen to English recordings and I read various texts in English. S19: Yes, I use my smartphone and tablet for out-of-class learning but I also use them during classes mostly to check words and collocations. S16: I think I do this during practical English language classes more regularly in the classroom than outside of it (...) In the classroom I check English words in digital dictionaries (...) I do this to check words, spelling, or to recall some words (...) or I use my smartphone to look for synonyms (...).
sections.The analysis of the gathered data also d: interviewees (13 or 65%) were in favor of using their smartphones and/or tablet computers for informal English learning (i.e. learning the target language for pleasure) and 7 (35%) students associated the use of their MobDs with formal learning (i.e. related to their studies). It should be noted, however, that only two interviewees claimed to hold and somewhat organize regular mobile English language sessions: S10: I think this is what I have talked about earlier, I mean these chats with my friends. Perhaps we don’t chat very regularly ... we chat three times a week and that’s it but, at the same time, it’s not sporadic because we arrange it and it takes place pretty regularly. S16: I often watch videos on YouTube and I do this the most often through my smartphone. Finally, it has to be noted that the use of mobile devices was not explicitly advised or suggested by the interviewees’ teachers during their practical English language classes or any other classes at the university. This is not to say, of course, that they never
sections.When asked to indicate the most frequent: means of mobile devices, all the interviewees indicated the target language vocabulary. In addition to this, some referred to pronunciation and only a few students mentioned grammar and practicing reading, listening and speaking skills. As far as practicing English vocabulary is concerned, the subjects chose to practice it through their smartphones and/or tables because they regarded this language subsystem as the most important to learn, they praised their MobDs for providing them with quick and easy access to needed words and see the way they were used in given sentences. As was stressed by many of the interviewees, learning English vocabulary by means of mobile devices also allowed them to check correct pronunciation of words (i.e. they listened to it or paid attention to phonetic transcription of words). The following excerpts exemplify the most typical usage of MobDs by the study participants: S3: (…) as for vocabulary I guess it’s much faster to search for words and know how to use them in sentences. S6: It’s easy and it’s very easy to look for words when I need them. S12: (…) I need vocabulary not only to communicate in English (…) when I look for words I look at contexts words are used (...) I always pay attention to spelling and also listen to pronunciation (How about phonetic transcription of words?) Phonetic transcription of words ... yes but not often unless audio is poor quality or it seems to sound somehow differently ... then I make sure how a word is pronounced and I read its phonetic transcription given there. As mentioned earlier, only a few students resorted to their MobDs in order to practice other language areas such as listening, reading and speaking as well as grammar. This is because they preferred more traditional resources (e.g. grammar books), they used other devices (e.g. laptop computers) or they regarded themselves as quite proficient in
sections.MobDs. Representative excerpts from the: S3: When it comes to grammar, for me it’s more convenient to use grammar books to learn it. S2: (…) I’m pretty good at English grammar and listening and I don’t have to use my smartphone to learn these language elements. S12: I think I’m quite good at grammar and I practice listening skills by means of my laptop computer.
sections.There is evidence that the use of mobile: English more and learn this language more effectively and efficiently (this advantageous effect was expressed by as many as 15 or 75% interviewees). This is because access to
sections.a smartphone or a tablet allows some lea: (S1: Yes, I think so. I think I spend more time ... If I was to use traditional materials, for example, books, I wouldn’t devote so much time to it.; S15: It seems to me that I dedicate more time to learn English this way and I learn more.), encouraged another student to learn more (S6: I’m more willing to use my smartphone than open a paper dictionary.) and allowed yet another subject to learn more vocabulary (S12: Yes, definitely. I wouldn’t have learned these words if I hadn’t used my phone.). Such
sections.beneficial outcomes of the use of MobDs: who said: If I’m to say that I devote more time for learning English it’s because I can devote more time to learning it ... in the way I compare a paper dictionary with an online one ... for example to check one word ... If I use a traditional dictionary it takes me longer, say three minutes, but If I use an online dictionary it takes me, say, ten seconds (...) this way I can devote less time to looking for
sections.It is also interesting to note that the: kinesthetic or tactical language learners: I think I spend more time … for me it’s much nicer and more interesting than sitting and reading books … it’s better for me since I’m kinesthetic so it’s hard for me to sit and read a traditional book ... it’s because I don’t remember then much but when I use my smartphone which is mobile I can ... I can do it while doing other activities and this makes things easier for me. (S5) Finally, it should be noted that 5 (25%) interviewees were not able to say whether or
sections.not the use of mobile devices made them: efficiently and they expressed their opinion by simply claiming that “It’s difficult to say”.
sections.The picture that emerges from the analys: learners' use of mobile devices for learning English is relatively encouraging. This is because all the study participants used, at least to some extent, their mobile devices (i.e. smartphones and/or tablet computers) in order to learn the English language autonomously. Moreover, the positive impact of using mobile devices for English study was acknowledged by the majority of the interviewees. Their beneficial contribution to
sections.their English development was chiefly li: resources, the opportunity to store them, comfort in using their smartphones and tablets anywhere and anytime as well as perceived gains in English learning. The results of the study also showed that all interviewees engaged with their smartphones and/or tablet computers to practice the target language vocabulary (plus some students also claimed to learn pronunciation of English words) and the majority of the subjects used
sections.their mobile devices autonomously in the: classes. Such a state of affairs can be explained in terms of increased awareness on the part of some students of the beneficial role of MobDs in foreign language learning, their
sections.ability to reach for appropriate tools a: goals and adjust their learning of the target language to their personal learning styles. Despite this positive view of MobDs reported by the study participants, the results of the
sections.study also revealed that only a few subj: master target language skills such as reading, listening, writing and speaking as well as English language grammar. In addition, some interviewees limited themselves to a
sections.rather intuitive and perhaps even sponta: language classroom. It should also be noted that almost half of the subjects regarded
sections.learning the English language despite th: them with the intention of learning English for years. Taking all these findings into account, one may conclude that this is due to a failure or underestimation of the role
sections.and place of mobile devices in foreign l: language teachers. It seems therefore warranted to say that the subjects’ use of mobile devices could be altered if teachers took into account the benefits they may offer. For this reason language teachers should, for instance, present the affordances of mobile technology and discuss them with students during language classes. They should also select mobile apps and create opportunities for using them in- and out-of-class learning
sections.by offering or designing tasks devoted t: subsystems suitable for the use of such devices. If this were to happen, teachers need
sections.to respond quickly to the constant and d: foreign/second language learning and teaching contexts by undergoing official teacher training not only in the area of technology-mediated language learning and teaching but also in the context of learner autonomy. As with all studies, the study reported in this paper has some limitations. Although the interviewees represented a range of experience of English language learning, the small number of participants reduces the generalizability of the results. Another limitation is related to the fact that the group was largely homogenous, i.e. the subjects came from the same institution and all studied English. Yet another weakness may concern the
sections.26: The EUROCALL Review, Volume 25, No. 2, September 2017 data collection instrument, namely the semi-structured interview which was conducted only once. Perhaps a different set of questions, their wording or a series of such interviews carried out over a particular period of time (say one academic year) may have yielded more detailed and insightful results. Despite these limitations, this study
sections.provided some insights into why and how: with their mobile devices to develop learning experiences. It should be stressed, however, that teacher involvement in creating conditions conducive to the use of mobile
sections.devices for language study may result in: technology (i.e. mobile devices) and, at the same time, may lead to greater students’ independence in learning the target language.

084e29ae-a0a7-490e-bcef-939adf6464b0.overview:
The EUROCALL Review, Volume 25, No. 2, September 2017
Research paper
A look at advanced learners’ use of mobile devices for
English language study: Insights from interview data
Mariusz Kruk
University of Zielona Gora, Poland
______________________________________________________________
mkruk @ uz.zgora.pl
Abstract
The paper discusses the results of a study which explored advanced learners of English
engagement with their mobile devices to develop learning experiences that meet their
needs and goals as foreign language learners. The data were collected from 20 students
by means of a semi-structured interview.

EXPERIMENT No. 04
Simulate the 3-DOF Articulated Robotic Manipulator
Name________________________________________ ID No.________________________
Sec. No _____________________Batch No._____________ Marks obtained_____________
Date________________________ Instructor’s signature_____________________________
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Objective:
To address the forward kinematics problem of a three-link planar 3-DOF articulated robotic
manipulator.
Introduction:
A 3-DOF (Degrees of Freedom) articulated robotic manipulator typically consists of three
joints that allow movement in three different ways. These degrees of freedom correspond to
movements in three-dimensional space, enabling the manipulator to position its end-effector in
various locations and orientations within its workspace. The joints in an articulated manipulator
are usually rotational (revolute), providing a high degree of flexibility and precision in its
movements, making it suitable for various industrial and research applications.
To address the forward kinematics problem of a three-link planar manipulator, we will use a
graphical depiction of the 3-DOF articulated robotic manipulator. Figure 1 illustrates a 3-DOF
planar robot manipulator situated in the x-z plane. This robotic arm has a fixed base and three
movable links of lengths Link 1, Link 2, and Link 3, which operate within the plane. The links
are interconnected by revolute joints with joint axes vertically aligned to the plane of the links.
Understanding the kinematics of the robot involves examining the relationship between the
joint angles and the resultant position and orientation of the end-effector. In a planar context,
this translates to understanding movements within a two-dimensional plane. Forward
kinematics involves determining the position and orientation of the end-effector based on
known joint angles. This process requires understanding the transformation matrices that relate
each link's coordinate frame to the base frame, with each joint angle contributing incrementally
to the overall transformation from the base to the end-effector.
Figure 1: The schematic of the 3-DOF robotic manipulator.

fb6a1e3e-f1c2-4db6-8aae-8fb4e765e566.text:
VOICE-NAV
NEURALNOVA
ED-TECH
BY
SOUMYA SILIVERI
VENKATA PALLAVI SIRIGIRI
JAYA PRANATHI JAGALAMARRI
MOHANA SRI AMBHIKA NANDYALA
PROBLEM STATEMENT
Limitations of Current Document Accessibility Tools
Visually impaired users commonly rely on screen readers to access digital documents such as PDFs, Word files, e-books, and online reports. While
these tools provide basic audio access, they primarily operate in a linear reading mode, offering limited support for selective navigation,
understanding, or revision.
Problem to Address
Difficulty navigating directly to relevant sections within long documents
No automatic overview or summarization to understand document content quickly
Limited voice control for stopping, repeating, or adjusting reading speed
Lack of voice-based bookmarking and note-taking for future reference
Minimal support for interactive clarification or learning assistance
As documents become longer and more complex, these limitations increase time consumption, cognitive effort, and dependence on
external assistance.
Observed Challenges
There is a need for a voice-first, accessibility-focused system that enables users to navigate, understand, personalize, and revisit document
content using natural spoken commands across multiple file formats.
02
PROPOSED SOLUTION
OBJECTIVES
KEY FEATURES
To enable visually impaired users to navigate documents using natural voice commands
To provide section-wise access and summarization instead of linear reading
Mic-activated, document-centric voice assistant
To support voice-based bookmarking and note-taking for future reference
Automatic 2–3 line audio overview after document
To allow users to control narration in real time (stop, repeat, speed adjustment)
or URL upload
To offer a document-aware voice assistant for clarification and revision
Voice-based section selection ,Real-time narration
To ensure accessibility across multiple document formats (PDF, Word, e-books, URLs)

04
UNIQUE SELLING PROPOSITION
Differentiation & Novelty
Existing screen readers focus on linear audio playback with limited support for section-wise navigation.
Most AI summarization tools are not designed for accessibility or voice-first interaction.
Current solutions lack memory, such as bookmarking, note storage, and revision support for visually impaired users.
Research Gaps
Voice-based section selection, summarization, and navigation, not just reading.
Integrated document-centric voice assistant instead of isolated tools.
Supports real-time control (stop, resume, speed adjustment) during narration.
Enables persistent notes and bookmarks accessible through voice.
Innovation
Combines speech processing, NLP, and LLMs into a single accessibility-focused workflow.
Provides a proactive document overview followed by fully user-controlled interaction.
Transforms documents into an interactive, voice-driven knowledge assistant rather than static audio output.
05
REAL WORLD APPLICATION
Real-Life Impact
Target Audience
Visually impaired and low-vision learners accessing Enables selective and efficient access to relevant
digital study material information through voice
Students and researchers working with long Reduces time and cognitive effort required to
academic documents understand long documents
Professionals handling reports, policies, and Supports independent learning, revision, and
technical documentation knowledge retention
Institutions seeking accessible document interaction Improves accessibility compliance and inclusive
solutions information access
Why This Matters
Transforms document access from passive listening into active understanding, helping users engage with information more
effectively and independently.
05

Extend the voice assistant to support regional and global languages for wider accessibility.
Offline & Low-Connectivity Mode
Enable basic document navigation and summaries without continuous internet access.
Advanced Conversational Learning
Improve the document assistant to support deeper clarification, step-by-step explanations, and guided
learning.
Adaptive Accessibility Personalization
Automatically adjust voice speed, tone, and interaction style based on user preferences and usage patterns.
Assistive Hardware Integration
Explore integration with Braille displays and accessibility devices for inclusive multi-modal access.
VoiceNav AI demonstrates how voice-first AI can move beyond basic screen reading to enable intelligent, interactive, and

7050d46e-1cc4-429d-aaff-826fd83dfca4.text:
ASSIGNMENT
ROBOTICS IN CONSTRUCTION
Submission Format
Name________________________________________ ID No.________________________
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Course/Subject: ________________________________ Department: ________________________________
Institution: ____________________________________ Submission Date: __________________________
Faculty Name: __________________________________ Signature: _________________________________
TABLE OF CONTENTS
1. Background (Importance, Concept, Motivation, Working Principle with Block Diagram/Architecture)
2. Literature Review (2024–2025; minimum 10 research papers)
3. BTL Levels (Detailed explanation of all 5 levels)
4. References (BibTeX format; font size 8)
1. BACKGROUND
1.1 Importance of Robotics in Construction
Construction is one of the world’s largest industries, yet it has historically lagged behind manufacturing in terms of productivity growth, automation maturity, and digitization. Projects are typically executed in unstructured, dynamic, and weather-affected environments, where work is distributed across multiple trades and schedules. These characteristics create variability in quality, delays, rework, and safety risks. Robotics addresses these challenges by bringing repeatability, precision, and data-driven decision-making to physical construction tasks.
Robots can operate continuously with consistent performance for repetitive tasks (e.g., drilling, fastening, layout marking, cutting, and bricklaying), and can be deployed in hazardous environments (e.g., demolition, confined spaces, high elevations, or areas with dust/toxic exposure). When combined with AI and sensing, robots can also support real-time inspection, progress monitoring, and defect detection, improving overall project control.
Improved safety by reducing human exposure to high-risk tasks.
Higher productivity through continuous operation and reduced downtime.
Better quality and reduced rework via precision execution and sensing-based feedback.
Addressing labor shortages by automating physically demanding and repetitive work.
Sustainability improvements through optimized material usage and reduced waste.
1.2 Concept and Definition
Robotics in construction refers to the application of autonomous or semi-autonomous machines and robotic systems to perform construction-related tasks. These tasks span the project lifecycle—from early-stage surveying and planning to onsite execution and long-term operation and maintenance. Construction robots may be stationary (e.g., robotic arms in prefabrication), mobile (wheeled/legged robots), aerial (UAVs/drones), or heavy-equipment-based (autonomous excavators, bulldozers, or haul trucks).
Unlike factory automation, construction sites are characterized by changing geometry, partial completion states, irregular materials, and frequent human presence. Therefore, successful construction robotics typically requires: (i) robust perception, (ii) safe motion planning and control, (iii) integration with digital models (BIM), and (iv) human–robot collaboration policies.
1.3 Motivation
Key motivations for adopting robotics in construction include the need to accelerate delivery timelines, mitigate skilled labor shortages, lower injury rates, and improve quality assurance. Rising costs and complex building geometries also push the industry toward automation-assisted methods such as robotic 3D concrete printing and robotic assembly. Additionally, digitized workflows (BIM, digital twins, and cloud-based project management) enable robots to connect physical tasks with planning and documentation, forming a cyber-physical construction ecosystem.
1.4 Working Principle
A generic construction robotic system follows a sense–plan–act loop. The system senses the environment (cameras, LiDAR, IMU, GPS/RTK, force-torque sensors), localizes itself, recognizes relevant objects/structures, and plans a safe and efficient action. It then executes the action using actuators (wheels, arms, hydraulic joints, end-effectors such as grippers, drillers, trowels, or print nozzles). Feedback is continuously used to correct deviations and ensure quality.
Robots may operate in three modes: (1) teleoperation (human directly controls the robot), (2) supervised autonomy (robot acts with human oversight), or (3) full autonomy (robot acts independently within constraints). Many real deployments use supervised autonomy to balance reliability and safety.
1.5 Block Diagram / Architecture
The following block diagram represents a typical robotics-in-construction architecture (robotic platform + AI + digital model integration):
In practice, the perception and control layers may run on edge devices (industrial PCs, embedded GPUs such as Jetson), while project-level analytics, documentation, and model training may run in the cloud. Safety modules enforce speed limits, geofences, emergency stops, and separation monitoring when humans are nearby.
1.6 Key Application Areas
Robotics is applied across multiple construction activities. Major application areas include:
Earthworks and heavy equipment automation (excavation, grading, hauling).
Masonry and finishing (robotic bricklaying, plastering, painting).
Concrete construction (robotic rebar tying, robotic formwork, 3D concrete printing).
Inspection and monitoring (UAV-based photogrammetry, defect detection, safety compliance).
Prefabrication and modular construction (robotic welding, cutting, assembly, and material handling).
Maintenance operations (facade inspection robots, cleaning robots, bridge inspection drones).
1.7 Safety and Standards Overview
Safety is central to construction robotics due to unpredictable environments and human proximity. A safe system typically implements: (i) functional safety (hardware interlocks, emergency stop, fault detection), (ii) operational safety (standard operating procedures, exclusion zones), and (iii) algorithmic safety (safe motion planning, speed and separation monitoring, constrained control policies). In collaborative scenarios, the robot’s control strategy may enforce limits on force/torque and motion to prevent injury.
1.8 AI/ML/DL/RL Roles in Construction Robotics
Artificial intelligence enables robots to convert raw sensor inputs into actionable decisions. Common uses include: deep learning for object detection and segmentation; machine learning for predictive maintenance and productivity estimation; reinforcement learning and imitation learning for complex control (e.g., excavation); and optimization algorithms for scheduling and path planning. AI also supports higher-level project tasks such as progress tracking against BIM and automated quality reporting.
Modern construction robots increasingly rely on multi-sensor fusion. For example, camera data provides rich semantic information (labels of objects and workers), while LiDAR offers robust geometry under varying lighting. Sensor fusion improves localization accuracy, supports obstacle detection, and enables safe navigation. When integrated with BIM, the robot can compare the as-planned model with as-built scans to detect deviations, estimate progress, and generate alerts. These capabilities shift robotics from isolated automation tools to integrated cyber-physical systems that continuously learn from site data.

2. LITERATURE REVIEW (2024–2025)
This literature review summarizes recent (2024–2025) research on construction robotics, focusing on the use of AI/ML/DL/RL and control strategies, highlighting key contributions, advancements, limitations, and future research directions. The papers are grouped by major application categories.
ExACT: An End-to-End Autonomous Excavator System Using Action Chunking With Transformers (arXiv, 2024)
AI/ML/DL/RL/Control strategy/Algorithm: Imitation learning with transformer-based action chunking; multimodal inputs (LiDAR, camera, joint states).
Contribution: Proposes an end-to-end policy mapping raw multimodal observations to excavator valve commands for reach/dig/dump behaviors.
Advancement: Moves beyond modular pipelines by learning compact action sequences, potentially reducing engineering overhead for autonomy.
Challenges/Limitations: Primarily validated in simulation; sim-to-real transfer, safety constraints, and robustness to soil variability remain open issues.
Future direction: Large-scale real-world trials with safety certification; combining imitation learning with online adaptation and constrained control.
Hybrid-driven autonomous excavator trajectory generation incorporating empirical driver skills and optimization (Automation in Construction, 2024)
AI/ML/DL/RL/Control strategy/Algorithm: Hybrid learning of expert trajectories + optimization-based planning for continuous excavator operation.
Contribution: Combines empirical driver skill modeling with optimization to generalize digging trajectories to new scenarios.
Advancement: Bridges human expertise with automated planning to improve practicality and smoothness of excavation motions.
Challenges/Limitations: Generalization may degrade under novel soil/tool interactions; high-quality demonstration data is required.
Future direction: Integrate online perception of soil state and RL-based adaptation for variable materials and terrain.
Reinforcement Learning-Based Bucket Filling for Autonomous Excavation (IEEE Transactions on Field Robotics, 2024)
AI/ML/DL/RL/Control strategy/Algorithm: Deep reinforcement learning for bucket filling; adaptive control under varying soil conditions with constraints.
Contribution: Develops an RL controller that adapts excavation behavior online without explicit soil parameter models.
Advancement: Demonstrates adaptability and constraint-aware control to avoid stalling and respect depth limits.
Challenges/Limitations: Training complexity and safety validation are challenging; deployment requires robust monitoring and fallback strategies.
Future direction: Combine RL with formal safety filters, domain randomization, and real-time terrain estimation.
Reinforcement learning-based trajectory planning for continuous excavation (Wiley (Computer-Aided Civil and Infrastructure Engineering / related), 2025)
AI/ML/DL/RL/Control strategy/Algorithm: Reinforcement learning for real-time trajectory planning in repeated excavation cycles; hybrid planning framework.
Contribution: Addresses continuous excavation planning with policies optimized for repeated operations and efficiency.
Advancement: Improves autonomy by targeting real-time planning rather than offline-only methods.
Challenges/Limitations: Performance depends on accurate environment representation; safe exploration is hard in real sites.
Future direction: Safe RL and human-in-the-loop learning for reliable field deployment.
Dynamic robotic bricklaying force-position control considering task constraints (Automation in Construction, 2025)
AI/ML/DL/RL/Control strategy/Algorithm: Force-position hybrid control; constraint-aware control for placement accuracy and stability.
Contribution: Proposes control strategies to regulate contact forces while maintaining brick placement precision.
Advancement: Supports robust bricklaying under variability by blending force feedback with position tracking.
Challenges/Limitations: Sensitive to calibration and on-site variability in mortar properties; integration with perception is needed.
Future direction: Closed-loop perception + adaptive control for varying mortar/workpiece properties and faster setup.
Safety-constrained Deep Reinforcement Learning control for human–robot collaboration in construction (Automation in Construction, 2025)
AI/ML/DL/RL/Control strategy/Algorithm: Deep RL with explicit safety constraints for collaborative tasks with humans.
Contribution: Introduces a DRL framework that accounts for safety limits during learning and execution for collaborative construction workflows.
Advancement: Moves toward practical HRC by embedding constraints into policy learning and control.
Challenges/Limitations: Data requirements and verifying safety across diverse site conditions remain difficult.
Future direction: Hybrid methods combining DRL with certified controllers and real-time human intention prediction.
A BIM-enabled robot control system for automated construction processes (International Journal of Construction Management, 2024)
AI/ML/DL/RL/Control strategy/Algorithm: BIM-to-robot control integration; model-based planning and execution; digital workflow integration.
Contribution: Connects BIM information to robot control to automate task execution and reduce manual programming.
Advancement: Improves interoperability and reduces setup time by leveraging as-planned digital models.
Challenges/Limitations: BIM inaccuracies and site changes can create execution errors without robust perception updates.
Future direction: Tighter BIM–digital twin loop with real-time scanning updates and semantic mapping.
Real-Time Precision in 3D Concrete Printing: Controlling Layer Width with Computer Vision and ML (Machines (MDPI), 2024)
AI/ML/DL/RL/Control strategy/Algorithm: Computer vision-based feedback; ML-assisted speed control for layer-width regulation.
Contribution: Presents a vision setup and control pipeline to adjust printing parameters in real time to achieve target geometry.
Advancement: Enables closed-loop correction, improving print quality and repeatability.
Challenges/Limitations: Camera occlusions, lighting, and material variability can reduce reliability.
Future direction: Sensor fusion (vision + laser profiling) and predictive control for complex geometries and outdoor conditions.
Towards full automation in 3D concrete printing construction (Developments in the Built Environment (Elsevier), 2024)
AI/ML/DL/RL/Control strategy/Algorithm: Sensor–printer integration; automated material testing and process parameterization for 3DCP.
Contribution: Proposes an integrated instrument and workflow supporting automated characterization and printing decisions.
Advancement: Improves end-to-end automation by linking material behavior to process settings.
Challenges/Limitations: Scaling and standardization across different mixes and climates is challenging.
Future direction: Standardized datasets and adaptive control frameworks for robust outdoor printing.
Investigation of Unsafe Construction Site Conditions Using UAV and Deep Learning (PPE/Helmet Monitoring) (Sensors (MDPI), 2024)
AI/ML/DL/RL/Control strategy/Algorithm: Deep learning vision models deployed with UAV imagery for safety compliance detection.
Contribution: Uses UAV monitoring to detect PPE compliance (e.g., helmets) and unsafe site conditions.
Advancement: Demonstrates scalable overhead safety monitoring without requiring wearable devices.
Challenges/Limitations: Privacy concerns, occlusions, and varying viewpoints may affect detection accuracy.
Future direction: Federated learning, privacy-preserving analytics, and multi-view tracking for robust deployment.
UAV-based deep learning applications for automated inspection: review and outlook (Automation in Construction, 2025)
AI/ML/DL/RL/Control strategy/Algorithm: Survey of deep learning for UAV inspection; autonomy, perception, and decision support.
Contribution: Provides structured overview of DL pipelines for inspection, segmentation, and damage assessment using UAV data.
Advancement: Highlights emerging trends toward autonomous flight, on-board inference, and automated reporting.
Challenges/Limitations: Data scarcity for rare defects; generalization across structures and environments remains limited.
Future direction: Benchmark datasets, domain adaptation, and standardized evaluation for practical inspection systems.
Defects inspection system for building facades using drones and deep learning techniques (Expert Systems with Applications, 2025)
AI/ML/DL/RL/Control strategy/Algorithm: CNN/encoder–decoder segmentation for multi-class facade defect detection; AI platform integration.
Contribution: Proposes an end-to-end drone-based facade inspection system with automated reporting.
Advancement: Moves from lab algorithms toward deployable platforms with APIs and reporting workflows.
Challenges/Limitations: Performance across different materials and lighting conditions may vary; annotation costs are high.
Future direction: Self-supervised learning, weak supervision, and continual learning for long-term deployment.
Real-time construction safety monitoring using a drone and advanced deep spatio-temporal models (Scientific Reports (Nature), 2025)
AI/ML/DL/RL/Control strategy/Algorithm: Deep spatio-temporal learning for safety event detection using drone video.
Contribution: Develops a system that captures temporal patterns to detect risky behaviors and conditions in real time.
Advancement: Improves beyond single-frame detection by using temporal context, enabling earlier alerts.
Challenges/Limitations: Reported performance may depend on dataset conditions; real-world generalization and compute constraints matter.
Future direction: Lightweight temporal models for on-board inference; standardized field trials with diverse sites.
2.1 Cross-Paper Analysis and Research Gaps
Across 2024–2025 research, a consistent pattern is the move from purely mechanized automation to perception-driven autonomy. Excavation research increasingly uses imitation learning and reinforcement learning to handle complex soil-tool interactions, while safety monitoring research leverages deep vision models to detect unsafe behaviors and PPE compliance from drone imagery.
A central technical gap is robust operation in unstructured environments. Construction sites change daily; materials deform; dust, rain, and lighting vary. Perception models trained on limited datasets may fail under distribution shifts. Therefore, domain adaptation, continual learning, and sensor fusion are critical.
Another gap is safety assurance. While safety-constrained RL and constrained control are promising, certification and validation remain difficult. Future systems will likely combine learned policies with hard safety filters, redundancy, and fail-safe behaviors.
Interoperability with BIM and digital twins is advancing, but still limited by inconsistent data standards and real-time update challenges. To close the loop, robots must align as-built perception with as-planned models and produce trustworthy progress/quality metrics.
Finally, there is a practical deployment gap: many studies validate in simulation or controlled environments. Real-world trials must address maintenance, operator training, site logistics, and integration with trade workflows.
Future research directions include: (i) multi-robot coordination for parallel work, (ii) foundation models for construction perception, (iii) safe learning with formal guarantees, (iv) standardized datasets and benchmarks for construction tasks, and (v) human-centered design that ensures robots augment workers rather than disrupt workflows. The most impactful systems will not only automate tasks, but also create consistent data trails that support quality assurance, scheduling optimization, and sustainability reporting.

3. BTL LEVELS (DETAILED EXPLANATION OF ALL 5 LEVELS)
BTL (Bloom’s Taxonomy Levels) categorize learning objectives from basic recall to higher-order thinking. Below are five commonly used levels in engineering education, with construction robotics examples for each.
BTL-1: Remember (Knowledge/Recall)
Definition: Ability to recall facts, terms, definitions, and basic concepts without necessarily understanding them.
Examples in ‘Robotics in Construction’:
List common types of construction robots (UAVs, bricklaying robots, autonomous excavators).
Define BIM, digital twin, SLAM, LiDAR, and end-effector.
Recall key benefits of robotics: safety, productivity, quality, waste reduction.
BTL-2: Understand (Comprehension)
Definition: Ability to explain ideas or concepts in one’s own words, interpret information, and summarize.
Examples in ‘Robotics in Construction’:
Explain why construction sites are harder for robots than factories (dynamic/unstructured environment).
Describe the sense–plan–act loop in construction robotics.
Summarize how drones collect data for inspection and progress tracking.
BTL-3: Apply (Application)
Definition: Ability to use learned information in new situations to solve problems or perform tasks.
Examples in ‘Robotics in Construction’:
Given a site map, choose sensors (camera/LiDAR/RTK) for robot localization and justify the selection.
Apply a basic PID/impedance control concept to maintain consistent contact force in robotic bricklaying.
Use BIM coordinates to generate a robot path for layout marking or drilling.
BTL-4: Analyze (Analysis)
Definition: Ability to break a problem into parts, detect relationships, and diagnose causes of errors.
Examples in ‘Robotics in Construction’:
Analyze failure modes of robot navigation on a dusty construction site (sensor noise, occlusion, wheel slip).
Compare modular autonomy vs end-to-end learning for excavator control and identify trade-offs.
Analyze inspection results to classify defects by severity and likely causes.
BTL-5: Evaluate (Evaluation/Judgment)
Definition: Ability to justify a decision based on criteria, test solutions, and assess alternatives.
Examples in ‘Robotics in Construction’:
Evaluate whether to use RL-based excavation control or model-based MPC given safety and data constraints.
Assess the ROI of deploying a bricklaying robot vs hiring additional labor for a mid-rise building project.
Judge the ethical and privacy implications of drone-based worker monitoring and propose mitigation measures.
In assignment writing, BTL mapping helps ensure that the report is not only descriptive but also analytical. For example, presenting a block diagram and definitions mostly targets BTL-1 and BTL-2, while designing an architecture for a given site scenario and selecting algorithms targets BTL-3. Discussing why systems fail under real site variability targets BTL-4, and recommending the best approach under cost, safety, and feasibility constraints targets BTL-5.
